agent_raw:
**Should the Pilot Results Support Full Expansion?**

No. The pilot was conducted in conditions that systematically differ from the proposed expansion target in multiple ways.

Store format: Twelve flagship stores with upgraded camera hardware are not representative of the full store portfolio including convenience-format locations and stores with legacy camera installations. Image quality, camera placement angles, store layout, and lighting conditions all differ.

Product category: Packaged snacks and beverages with simple, uniform planogram layouts present a much more tractable computer vision problem than the proposed expansion categories. Fresh produce requires detecting OOS in irregular, varied inventory presentations. Bakery items have high visual variability by definition. The detection model was validated on a fundamentally different visual domain.

Ground truth: Store staff who knew the pilot was under evaluation conducted the manual audits used as reference labels. Observer awareness tends to improve task performance, creating reference audits that are more thorough than what steady-state operations would produce.

A decision to expand to all store formats and complex categories should be preceded by a stratified expansion pilot that includes convenience-format stores, stores with older hardware, and at minimum one complex category (fresh produce or bakery), with audits designed to minimize observer effects.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Store format not representative — flagship stores only
Issue 2: Category generalization failure — easy packaged goods vs. fresh/bakery
Issue 3: Ground truth observer effect — informed staff
